worth_it_explanation: Demand routing decides which demand source gets the chance to fill each video slot and in what order, so it controls whether the highest-value demand wins the impressions it should and whether lower-value house or remnant ads stay out of the way of paid demand. A routing setup that was assembled one demand partner at a time, without a deliberate priority scheme, can let programmatic starve direct-sold inventory or let house ads clear slots that paid demand would have filled. The check is a review of the priority and routing rules a publisher already has in SpringServe, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete routing decision the publisher can test.
intro: Demand routing in SpringServe decides which demand source competes for each video or Connected TV (CTV) slot and in what order those sources are tried, across direct-sold campaigns, programmatic demand, and house ads. This subcategory is about the priority and routing scheme itself, the order and the competition rules that govern an ad request, rather than the individual demand tags or the floor prices they carry. These checks confirm that the routing is a deliberate, value-aware plan rather than a stack of demand sources added over time, and that it neither starves programmatic demand nor lets house ads displace paid impressions.

audit_checks.title: Demand priority and routing are not a deliberate, value-aware plan
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When demand sources are stacked into the routing without a deliberate priority that reflects what each source is worth, higher-value direct-sold or premium programmatic demand can lose slots to lower-value sources tried ahead of it. The result is impressions clearing below the price they could have reached, which is revenue left uncollected on inventory the publisher already controls.

educate.base:
When a video player asks SpringServe to fill an ad break, SpringServe works through the demand sources eligible for that slot in an order the publisher has configured. That order, together with the rules that decide which source competes and which wins, is the demand routing. It spans direct-sold campaigns sold at a fixed rate, programmatic demand bidding in real time, and house ads the publisher runs to fill what nothing else takes. The routing is what turns a single ad request into a winner.

The risk is not that any one demand source is wrong. The risk is a routing scheme that grew one demand partner at a time, with each source added where it happened to fit rather than where its value places it. A direct-sold campaign carries a committed rate the publisher already agreed to deliver, so it usually deserves first claim on the slots it targets. Premium programmatic demand may clear higher than remnant demand and deserves to be tried ahead of it. House ads carry no revenue and belong last, as a fallback. A routing order that does not reflect those value differences can let a lower-value source win a slot that a higher-value source would have filled.

SpringServe expresses this through the priority and competition controls on its demand tags and campaigns, and the underlying ad serving follows the IAB Tech Lab video standards. The Video Multiple Ad Playlist (VMAP) defines the structure of the ad breaks in the content, including how many ads each break allows, and the Video Ad Serving Template (VAST) defines how an ad server delegates a slot or a pod to the demand that fills it. The routing decides which demand reaches those breaks first.

A few signs point to routing that was assembled rather than planned:
  • Demand sources sit at the same priority with no rule reflecting that direct-sold or premium demand should win ahead of remnant demand
  • The publisher cannot state the intended order of direct-sold, programmatic, and house demand for a given slot
  • Reporting shows higher-value demand losing slots to lower-value sources that are tried ahead of it
You may want to confirm that the priority order reflects what each demand source is worth. One approach is to map the intended order for a representative slot, then read the win data to see whether the routing delivers it.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a streaming service or broadcaster, direct-sold sponsorships and premium programmatic deals usually carry committed rates and brand commitments that should win the slots they target before open-market demand competes. You may want to confirm the routing gives those committed deals first claim on their slots, so a remnant source does not win a break that a sponsor paid to hold.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-content video property, sponsored product placements and shoppable video segments often carry higher-value direct deals tied to a specific show or product. You may want to confirm the routing places those direct deals ahead of open programmatic demand on the slots they were sold against, rather than letting both compete on equal footing.

educate.learn_more_note: SpringServe's detailed configuration documentation for demand priority and routing is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the priority order across the demand sources eligible for a representative slot, and look for any rule that reflects the value differences between direct-sold, premium programmatic, remnant, and house demand.

springserve_console.label: SpringServe console
steps: Open the demand tags and campaigns eligible for a representative video or Connected TV slot, and list them with their assigned priority.
For each source, note whether it is direct-sold, programmatic, remnant, or house, and at what priority it sits relative to the others.
Record whether the priority order reflects the value of each source, with committed direct-sold and premium programmatic demand ahead of remnant demand and house ads last.
Ask the publisher to state the intended routing order for that slot, and record whether the configured order matches the stated one.

springserve_reporting.label: SpringServe reporting
steps: Build a report for the slot broken out by demand source, with win rate, impressions, and average revenue per impression.
Compare which sources win the slot against the intended priority order, and note any case where a lower-value source wins ahead of a higher-value one.
Note whether the win data supports the current routing order or whether it points to a priority that does not reflect source value.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for a representative slot, the priority assigned to each eligible demand source and whether that order reflects the value of the source, alongside the win data showing which sources actually win. A routing scheme where direct-sold and premium demand sit ahead of remnant and house demand, and the win data matches the intended order, passes. A scheme where the priority does not reflect source value, or where the win data shows lower-value demand winning ahead of higher-value demand, is a fail recorded with the slot and the sources affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the priority assigned to each demand source and the win data for the slot above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} routes demand into its video slots without a deliberate priority that reflects what each demand source is worth, so higher-value direct-sold and premium programmatic demand can lose slots to lower-value sources tried ahead of it. The routing decides which demand wins each ad break, and an order that grew one partner at a time rather than by value leaves impressions clearing below the price they could have reached. {{voice_recommend}} mapping the intended order of direct-sold, programmatic, and house demand for a representative slot in SpringServe, then reading the win data by source to confirm the routing delivers that order. After the change, recheck win rate and average revenue per impression to confirm the higher-value demand wins the slots it should.

audit_checks.title: House ads or remnant demand displace paid demand in the routing
audit_checks.impact_rationale: House ads carry no revenue, so any paid impression they win is revenue lost outright rather than merely underpriced. When house or remnant demand sits high enough in the routing to clear slots that programmatic or direct-sold demand would have filled, the publisher gives away inventory that had paying demand waiting for it, which is a direct loss on inventory the publisher already controls.

educate.base:
House ads are the ads a publisher runs to fill a slot when nothing else takes it, promoting the publisher's own content, a newsletter signup, or a sponsor commitment that carries no auction revenue. Remnant demand is the low-value programmatic demand a publisher accepts to keep fill high. Both have a place at the bottom of the routing as a fallback. The problem starts when either one sits high enough in the priority to win slots that paying demand would have filled.

The cost here is sharper than an underpriced impression. A house ad clears a slot at zero revenue, so a paid impression it displaces is revenue lost outright, not revenue traded down. Remnant demand clears at a low price, so a premium impression it wins is the gap between the low clearing price and the higher price the premium demand would have paid. In both cases the publisher had paying demand available for the slot and the routing handed it to a lower-value or no-value source instead.

This usually traces to one of two routing mistakes. A house campaign set to a high priority or a guaranteed delivery goal can outrank the programmatic and direct-sold demand it was meant to backstop. Or programmatic demand can be starved upstream, given too little time to respond or too few of the opportunities, so the slot falls through to house or remnant fill before the paying demand had a fair chance. In the IAB Tech Lab OpenRTB standard, each bid response can carry one or more seat bids competing for the impression, and that competition only protects the publisher's revenue when programmatic demand actually reaches the auction before a fallback claims the slot.

A few signs point to fallback demand displacing paid demand:
  • A house campaign sits at a priority that lets it win ahead of programmatic or direct-sold demand rather than only when those sources return nothing
  • House ads or remnant demand show a fill share larger than the unsold inventory alone would explain
  • Programmatic demand is given a short timeout or a small share of opportunities, so slots fall through to fallback fill before the bids arrive
You may want to confirm that house and remnant demand win only the slots paid demand declined. One approach is to read the fill by source against the unsold rate, then check that house priority and the programmatic timeout leave paying demand a fair chance to win first.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a streaming service or broadcaster, house promotions for upcoming programming or in-network content can be set to deliver against a goal, which can push them ahead of paid demand if the priority is too high. You may want to confirm house promotions win only the slots that paid sponsorship and programmatic demand declined, rather than clearing breaks that had paying demand waiting.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-content video property, house ads promoting the publisher's own products or sales can crowd out paid demand if they sit high in the routing. You may want to confirm those self-promotions fill only the unsold slots, so a shoppable segment with paying demand available is not handed to a zero-revenue house ad.

educate.learn_more_note: SpringServe's detailed configuration documentation for house campaign priority and programmatic timeout settings is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review where house ads and remnant demand sit in the routing relative to paid demand, and check whether programmatic demand is given a fair chance to respond before a fallback claims the slot.

springserve_console.label: SpringServe console
steps: Open the house campaigns and remnant demand tags, and record the priority each sits at relative to the programmatic and direct-sold demand for the same slots.
Note whether any house campaign carries a delivery goal or a priority that lets it win ahead of paid demand rather than only as a fallback.
Record the programmatic timeout and the share of opportunities programmatic demand is given, and note whether either is short enough to let slots fall through to fallback fill before bids arrive.

springserve_reporting.label: SpringServe reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by demand source with fill share and revenue per impression, separating house ads, remnant demand, programmatic, and direct-sold.
Compare the house and remnant fill share against the rate of genuinely unsold inventory, and note any case where fallback fill is larger than the unsold rate alone would explain.
Note whether the data shows house or remnant demand winning slots that paid demand was available to fill.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the priority of house and remnant demand relative to paid demand, the programmatic timeout and opportunity share, and the fill share by source against the unsold rate. A setup where house and remnant demand sit below paid demand and win only the slots paid demand declined, with programmatic given a fair chance to respond, passes. A setup where house or remnant demand outranks paid demand, or where a short programmatic timeout lets slots fall to fallback fill before bids arrive, is a fail recorded with the campaigns and slots affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the priority of house and remnant demand, the programmatic timeout, and the fill share by source above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that house ads or remnant demand on {{site_name}} can win video slots that paid demand was available to fill. A house ad clears a slot at zero revenue, so a paid impression it displaces is revenue lost outright, and a short programmatic timeout can let slots fall to fallback fill before the bids arrive. {{voice_recommend}} setting house and remnant demand below paid demand in the SpringServe routing so they win only the slots paid demand declined, and confirming the programmatic timeout and opportunity share leave paying demand a fair chance to win first. After the change, recheck the fill share by source against the unsold rate to confirm fallback demand is filling only what paid demand left.
